tweets.text:
We 'fat' pitched long Colombia in late '24 into '25 & the premise was straightforward:

- Equity markets were trading at historically depressed valuations, offering attractive carry & dividend yields that effectively paid investors to wait.

- At the same time, we expected the U.S. dollar to weaken during a Trump presidency, creating a natural tailwind for non-U.S. assets.

- And most importantly, we wanted to position ahead of what we believed would be a broader political rotation across the region, as the market began to front-run a shift from left-leaning governments toward more market-friendly, right-wing leadership. After many investors missed Argentina in late '23, we saw the rest of Latin America as the next logical expression of that trade (Chile / Colombia), with a meaningful re-rating once the shift became clearer. Within that framework, Colombia stood out as our favorite setup amongst Chile, Colombia, Peru, & Brazil.
tweets.full_text:
We 'fat' pitched long Colombia in late '24 into '25 & the premise was straightforward:

- Equity markets were trading at historically depressed valuations, offering attractive carry & dividend yields that effectively paid investors to wait.

- At the same time, we expected the U.S. dollar to weaken during a Trump presidency, creating a natural tailwind for non-U.S. assets.

- And most importantly, we wanted to position ahead of what we believed would be a broader political rotation across the region, as the market began to front-run a shift from left-leaning governments toward more market-friendly, right-wing leadership. After many investors missed Argentina in late '23, we saw the rest of Latin America as the next logical expression of that trade (Chile / Colombia), with a meaningful re-rating once the shift became clearer. Within that framework, Colombia stood out as our favorite setup amongst Chile, Colombia, Peru, & Brazil.

tweets.text:
Colombia has now finally joined the right-wing contagion underway across all of LatAm following the victory by Espriella.

With the political backdrop increasingly shifting toward pro-business governments, and with the region remaining one of the most underinvested globally despite being in the midst of a commodity upcycle, the LatAm boom likely still has plenty of runway ahead.
